page.content: In the analysis of diabetes classification, the data table provides critical insights into the misclassification of Type 1 and Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus (T1DM and T2DM) among older populations. Notably, the column labeled "Absolute value" highlights that older individuals with T2DM have a mean age of 62 years, contrasting sharply with the younger mean age of 47 years for those accurately diagnosed with T1DM. This age discrepancy is significant and underscores the potential for misclassification, particularly in clinical settings where age-related factors may influence diagnostic criteria.

Diabetes Mellitus (T1DM and T2DM) among older populations. Notably, the column labeled "Absolute value" highlights that
older individuals with T2DM have a mean age of 62 years, contrasting sharply with the younger mean age of 47 years for those
accurately diagnosed with T1DM. This age discrepancy is significant and underscores the potential for misclassification,
particularly in clinical settings where age-related factors may influence diagnostic criteria.

Summary of audit data
FindingQuantitative basis of finding
Older T2DM people are more likely to be misclassified as T1DMMean age 62 years vs, 47 years for people with true T1DM
Misclassified T1DM people have lower HbA1c than true T1DMTrue T1DM 8.5 vs 7.7 misclassified T1DM (paired t test p = 0.029)
Correctly diagnosed T2DM people tend to have increases in weight and falls in their HbA1cBMI increases from 28.4 to 29.2 (not significant), HbA1c falls from 5.7 to 5.3 (p < 0.001)
Miscoded people are managed suboptimallyMean HbA1c significantly lower in patients on the disease register (HbA1c, SEM 0.11 vs. HbA1c 8.1 SEM = 0.42, p = 0.006)
Those people on a disease register have significant improvements in their HbA1c reductionFrom HbA1c 7.6 (SEM = 0.14) to 7 (SEM = 0.12) t test p < 0.001)

cells.cell_text: Older T2DM people are more likely to be misclassified as T1DM
Mean age 62 years vs, 47 years for people with true T1DM
Misclassified T1DM people have lower HbA1c than true T1DM
True T1DM 8.5 vs 7.7 misclassified T1DM (paired t test p = 0.029)
Correctly diagnosed T2DM people tend to have increases in weight and falls in their HbA1c
BMI increases from 28.4 to 29.2 (not significant), HbA1c falls from 5.7 to 5.3 (p < 0.001)
Mean HbA1c significantly lower in patients on the disease register (HbA1c, SEM 0.11 vs. HbA1c 8.1 SEM = 0.42, p = 0.006)
Those people on a disease register have significant improvements in their HbA1c reduction
From HbA1c 7.6 (SEM = 0.14) to 7 (SEM = 0.12) t test p < 0.001)

page.content: The "Standardized value" column further quantifies this phenomenon, indicating a substantial variance in misclassification rates, which is crucial for refining diagnostic practices. With a total variance of 100%, this suggests that the age factor plays a pivotal role in distinguishing between T1DM and T2DM. The findings underscore the necessity for healthcare professionals to adopt more nuanced approaches in diabetes classification, especially for older patients, to mitigate the risk of misdiagnosis. This is particularly relevant in light of the observed trends that indicate a higher likelihood of misclassification among older T2DM patients, which could lead to inappropriate treatment strategies and adverse health outcomes.
